name: Indiana Office of Technology (IOT)
tagline: Indiana's central IT agency: IOT runs enterprise infrastructure, cybersecurity, and data services for state government — funded almost entirely by chargebacks to the agencies it serves, while statewide IT buying flows through IDOA's Quantity Purchase Agreements.
overview: The Indiana Office of Technology (IOT) is the state's central IT agency, delivering shared infrastructure, networking, cloud, cybersecurity, and data services to dozens of executive-branch agencies from Indianapolis. IOT is best understood as a service organization rather than a large discretionary spender: it is a roughly 450-person agency with an operating budget reported around $250M, funded almost entirely through chargebacks — it bills the agencies it serves for the services they consume rather than drawing a large general-revenue appropriation. That model shapes the buying motion: most large agencies (Health, Transportation, Revenue, FSSA) keep their own IT teams and budgets, while IOT owns enterprise platforms, the state network, the security perimeter, and data services. Statewide commodity and IT purchasing is competed and held by the Indiana Department of Administration (IDOA) through Quantity Purchase Agreements (QPAs) that agencies — and many local governments — can buy from directly. Separately, Indiana has become a national data-center magnet, anchored by the LEAP Innovation and Research District in Lebanon, where Meta broke ground on a $10B+ AI data-center campus in February 2026 — context that frames Indiana's broader AI and infrastructure ambitions even though those private hyperscale builds sit outside IOT's own footprint.

budget.intro: IOT does not run on a large appropriation — it recovers its costs by billing the agencies it serves. The state's 2025–2027 biennial budget is roughly $46B (about $23.0B FY2026 / $23.2B FY2027), but IOT's own operating budget is reported around $250M, almost all of it chargeback-funded. A statewide fiscal squeeze is relevant: in 2025 the State Budget Agency ordered agencies to absorb a combined ~10% reduction (a 5% cut plus an additional 5% withholding) after a weak May 2025 revenue forecast.

stats.label: IOT operating budget
stats.note: Reported figure; funded almost entirely via chargebacks to served agencies

stats.label: 2025 agency reductions
stats.note: 5% cut + 5% withholding ordered after a weak May 2025 revenue forecast

notes: Chargeback funding means IOT has limited discretionary room — its CIO has publicly argued for a hybrid model (appropriation + service billing) to fund R&D and experimentation.
The largest agency IT budgets sit with the agencies themselves (Health, FSSA, Transportation, Revenue); IOT owns the shared platforms, network, and security layer.
The 2025 statewide reductions pressure discretionary IT spend — expect scrutiny on new commitments and a bias toward consolidation and cost recovery.

initiatives.name: IN AI / human-centered AI adoption
initiatives.summary: Gov. Braun announced 'IN AI' on April 28, 2026 — a statewide push to make Indiana an AI-ready state, executed largely through the Central Indiana Corporate Partnership (CICP) for business adoption. Inside government, IOT has run a Microsoft Copilot pilot to evaluate productivity and data-protection. AI is a stated priority but governance and tooling are still maturing.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity & data protection
initiatives.summary: IOT owns the state's enterprise security perimeter under a long-tenured CISO and coordinates broadly through the Indiana Executive Council on Cybersecurity (IECC). Security remains a steady, board-level priority across endpoint, network, identity, and data-protection — a durable buying center.

initiatives.name: Digital services & citizen experience
initiatives.summary: A new Chief Digital Experience Officer (2026) is leading state digital-service strategy, including the in.gov homepage chatbot as an early citizen-engagement tool. Modernizing front-door digital services is an emerging track.

initiatives.name: LEAP district & data-center economy
initiatives.summary: Indiana's LEAP Innovation and Research District (Lebanon) anchors a wave of hyperscale AI data-center investment — Meta broke ground on a $10B+ campus in Feb 2026. These are private builds, not IOT projects, but they signal the state's AI-infrastructure direction and power/utility planning.

procurement.intro: Selling into Indiana state government runs largely on IDOA's competed Quantity Purchase Agreements (QPAs), not one-off agency RFPs. Two things to confirm first: that Dell holds (or is on) the relevant IT QPAs so agencies can buy directly, and which buying center owns a given workload — IOT for shared enterprise/network/security, the agency itself for its own systems.

vehicles.name: IDOA Quantity Purchase Agreements (QPAs)
vehicles.detail: The primary vehicle — competitively established statewide contracts that pre-qualify vendors by commodity. Agencies (and many cities, towns, and political subdivisions where the QPA allows) can buy directly without a separate solicitation.

vehicles.name: IOT Services Catalog / chargeback
vehicles.detail: IOT delivers shared infrastructure, network, cloud, and security as billed services to agencies. Influencing IOT's platform standards shapes what flows downstream to served agencies.

vehicles.name: Cooperative / national vehicles
vehicles.detail: Indiana public entities can also leverage national cooperative purchasing vehicles for IT where applicable — confirm allowability per buyer alongside the in-state QPA path.

powerStructure.name: Warren Lenard
powerStructure.title: State CIO & IOT Director
powerStructure.note: Appointed by Gov. Braun in March 2025. The top relationship for the IOT motion; publicly advocating a hybrid funding model to fund innovation. Background in private-sector IT (most recently automotive financial services).

powerStructure.name: Hemant Jain
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
powerStructure.note: At IOT since May 2018; long-tenured, nationally recognized security leader. Owns the state security perimeter and standards — the security buying center.

powerStructure.name: Dave Fox
powerStructure.title: Chief Technology Officer (CTO)
powerStructure.note: At IOT since Nov 2020. Owns enterprise technology architecture and platform direction — a key technical relationship for infrastructure and cloud.

powerStructure.name: Chris Henderson
powerStructure.title: Chief Relationship & Delivery Officer
powerStructure.note: Joined IOT in 2026 with two-plus decades of state government and consulting experience; owns agency relationships and service delivery — a route into demand across served agencies.

powerStructure.name: Graig Lubsen
powerStructure.title: Chief Digital Experience Officer
powerStructure.note: Named in 2026; leads digital-service strategy and delivery for the state — the owner for citizen-facing modernization and chatbot/AI front-door work.

news.title: Gov. Braun announces 'IN AI' to make Indiana an AI-ready state
news.impact: Statewide AI priority (business adoption via CICP) raises executive air-cover for AI; align infrastructure and productivity plays to the agenda.

news.title: Meta breaks ground on $10B+ AI data-center campus at LEAP district, Lebanon
news.impact: Signals Indiana's AI-infrastructure trajectory and power/utility planning; private build, but reinforces the state's data-center economy narrative.

news.title: New IOT leadership seats filled (CRDO, CDEO) under CIO Lenard
news.impact: Fresh leadership in relationship/delivery and digital experience — re-map the buying center and rebuild relationships early.

news.title: Statewide ~10% agency reductions take hold after weak revenue forecast
news.impact: Discretionary IT spend is under pressure; lead with consolidation, cost-recovery, and clear ROI rather than net-new commitments.

news.title: Warren Lenard appointed State CIO & IOT Director by Gov. Braun
news.impact: New top relationship for the IOT motion; private-sector CIO advocating a hybrid funding model — frame innovation in cost-recovery terms.

dellActions: Confirm Dell's position on the relevant IDOA IT QPAs — it's the direct-buy access path for agencies (and many locals), not just IOT itself.
Build the top relationship with CIO Warren Lenard and frame infrastructure/AI value in cost-recovery terms that fit IOT's chargeback model and the 2025 budget squeeze.
Lead with AI-ready infrastructure and security through CTO Dave Fox and CISO Hemant Jain — the durable enterprise and security buying centers.

Tie the pitch to the 'IN AI' agenda and the LEAP data-center narrative, positioning Dell as the on-prem/hybrid AI-infrastructure partner for state workloads (distinct from the hyperscale private builds).
Engage the new CRDO (Chris Henderson) and CDEO (Graig Lubsen) early to surface modernization and digital-service demand across served agencies.
